California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Richard E. Archibald 
Deputy City Attorney 
october 10, 1989 
812 Tenth street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814-2694 
Dear Mr. Archibald: 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. I-89-573 
You have requested advice on behalf of the Sacramento City 
Council regarding their obligations under the mass mailing 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")l/, as amended 
by Proposition 73. 
Since your request does not involve a specific 
factual situation we are treating your request as one for informal 
assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) .2/ 
Please note that the Commission is in the process of revising 
Regulation 18901, which implements the statutory mass mailing 
restrictions. 
We anticipate noticing amendments to the regulation 
in October 1989, and adopting those amendments at the 
December 13, 1989 Commission meeting. 
A draft of the proposed 
amendments is enclosed. 
until the amendments are formally adopted 
by the Commission, the current version of Regulation 18901 remains 
in force. 
OUr analysis is based upon the current version of the 
regulation. 
QUESTIONS 
1. 
May the city send at public expense, notices of town hall 
meetings identifying the elected official who will be present? 
1/ 
Government Code Sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations 
Section 18000, et seq. 
All references to regulations are to Title 
2, Division 6 ot the California Code of Regulations. 
2/ 
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the 
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. 
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329 (c) (3).) 


Our File No. 1-89-573 
Page 2 
2. 
May such notices be sent out on the individual letterhead 
of the mayor and councilmembers? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. 
The city may send at public expense, notices of meetings 
which directly relate to an elected official's incumbent 
governmental duties, and which solely include the time, date, 
place, a concise description of the subject matter of the meeting, 
and one mention of the elected'official's name. 
2. 
Such notices may be sent out on the individual letterhead 
of the mayor and councilmembers. 
ANALYSIS 
section 89001 of the Political Reform Act, as amended by 
Proposition 73, provides that "no newsletter or other mass mailing 
shall be sent at public expense." 
A mass mailing is defined in 
Section 82041.5 as two hundred or more substantially similar 
pieces of mail. 
The Commission has determined that the intent of 
the voters in adopting Proposition 73 was to prevent elected 
officers from gaining an advantage from incumbency by using public 
funds to send out newsletters and other mass mailings which 
increase their name recognition. 
(Regulation 18901, copy 
enclosed.) 
Regulation 18901(c) (2) (ii) sets out the general rule that a 
mass mailing sent out by an elected officer may not include the 
elected officer's name, office, photograph or other reference to 
the elected officer. 
Regulation 18901(f) lists exceptions to the 
general rule. 
specifically, Regulation 18901(f) (8) provides an 
exception for: 
(8) 
Mailings sent to the elected officer's 
constituents which directly relate to that elected 
official's incumbent governmental duties and which 
solely include the time, date, place, and a concise 
description of the subject matter of a public 
meeting to be held by the elected officer; 
The name of the elected officer may be used once in the 
notice in order to identify the officer holding the meeting. 
However, the mailing may not be signed by the elected officer. 
(Andersen Advice Letter No. A-89-273, copy enclosed.) 


Our File No. 1-89-573 
Page 3 
Regulation 18901(e) provides another exception to the mass 
mailing prohibition provides: 
(e) 
A newsletter or other mass mailing is not 
prohibited by Government Code section 89001 if it 
meets all of the following criteria: 
(1) 
The stationery, forms and envelopes 
used for the mailing are the standard 
stationery, forms and envelopes of the agency 
or committee of the agency; and 
(2) 
The name of an elected officer who 
is affiliated with the agency or committee 
appears, in the standard letterhead of 
logotype of the stationery, forms or envelopes 
of the agency, a committee of the agency, or 
the elected official and the newsletter or 
mass mailing is not otherwise prohibited under 
subdivision (c). 
As used in this subdivision, the term 
"letterhead or logotype" includes a listing of 
agency or committee officials or members, in 
which all who are listed appear in the same 
typeface and type size and location in the 
layout of the newsletter or other mass 
mailing. 
As used in this subdivision, the term 
"standard letterhead or logotype" refers to 
any regularly used by the agency, subunit or 
the elected officer ... 
Using this exception, a notice 
subdivision (f) (8), may be sent out 
or envelopes as provided for above. 
A-89-132, copy enclosed.) 
of the type provided for under 
on standard stationery, forms 
(stirling Advice Letter, No. 
If you have any further questions, please contact me at (916) 
322-5901. 
KED/JGM/aa 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
Genera~l 
cO"unsel

J

Counsel, Legal Division

This office has received requests from City officials and 
staff concerning the meaning and effect of the "mass mailings" 
provisions of Proposition 73 and the implementing regulations. 
While I have discussed some of these matters with representatives 
of your office over the telephone, 
I 
would like to request, 
pursuant to Government Code Section 83114, an opinion or, alterna-
tively, a written advice letter, on some of these questions. 
Pursuant to Government 
Code Section 
83114, 
this 
respectfully requests on behalf of the City of Sacramento, 
elected and appointed officials, agents and employees, an 
or written response addressing the following questions: 
office 
and its 
opinion 
1. 
Prior to the enactment of Proposition 73 and the adoption 
of the implementing regulations, the City of Sacramento has held 
"town hall meetings" at various locations throughout the city for 
the purpose of discussing matters of general concern or, in some 
instances, particular events, projects and issues. 
These meetings 
are not mandatory pursuant to any federal, state or local law, and 
are held for the purpose of disseminating or gathering information 
or opinions about various issues or topics of concern. 
Elected 
officials mayor may not be present at these "town hall meetings." 


Kathryn Donovan 
FPPC 
August 9, 1989 
Page Two 
(a) 
May the City send, at public expense, notices of these 
"town hall meetings" to 200 or more people if such notices identify 
the elected official(s) who will be present? 
I have assumed that 
community meeting notices which do not contain any reference to an 
elected official are permissible pursuant to 2 Cal.Admin. Code 
§18901(f) (8) or (f) (10). 
If this assumption is incorrect, please 
advise. 
(b) 
Assuming that the notices referenced in (a), above, are 
permissible, which, if any, of the four sample notices attached as 
Exhibits 1 through 4 hereto are permissible? 
2. 
The City of Sacramento does not have a standard letterhead 
on which the names of the Mayor and eight Councilmembers are 
listed. 
Instead, each Councilmember and the Mayor has his or her 
own standard letterhead, a copy of which is attached hereto as 
Exhibit 5. 
(a) 
May the community meeting notices be sent out on the 
letterhead of the Councilmember who is calling the community 
meeting? 
Your prompt response to the foregoing questions would be 
greatly appreciated. 
If you have any questions, or if further 
explanation is needed, please do not hesitate to contact the 
undersigned. 
REAjjmv 
Attachments 
Very truly yours,

2/

CONCLUSION: 1.

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission Richard E. Archibald Deputy City Attorney october 10, 1989 812 Tenth street Sacramento, CA 95814-2694 Dear Mr. Archibald: Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-89-573 You have requested advice on behalf of the Sacramento City Council regarding their obligations under the mass mailing provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")l/, as amended by Proposition 73. Since your request does not involve a specific factual situation we are treating your request as one for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) .2/ Please note that the Commission is in the process of revising Regulation 18901, which implements the statutory mass mailing restrictions. We anticipate noticing amendments to the regulation in October 1989, and adopting those amendments at the December 13, 1989 Commission meeting. A draft of the proposed amendments is enclosed. until the amendments are formally adopted by the Commission, the current version of Regulation 18901 remains in force. OUr analysis is based upon the current version of the regulation. QUESTIONS 1. May the city send at public expense, notices of town hall meetings identifying the elected official who will be present? 1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 ot the California Code of Regulations. 2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329 (c) (3).) Our File No. 1-89-573 Page 2 2. May such notices be sent out on the individual letterhead of the mayor and councilmembers? CONCLUSIONS 1. The city may send at public expense, notices of meetings which directly relate to an elected official's incumbent governmental duties, and which solely include the time, date, place, a concise description of the subject matter of the meeting, and one mention of the elected'official's name. 2. Such notices may be sent out on the individual letterhead of the mayor and councilmembers. ANALYSIS section 89001 of the Political Reform Act, as amended by Proposition 73, provides that "no newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at public expense." A mass mailing is defined in Section 82041.5 as two hundred or more substantially similar pieces of mail. The Commission has determined that the intent of the voters in adopting Proposition 73 was to prevent elected officers from gaining an advantage from incumbency by using public funds to send out newsletters and other mass mailings which increase their name recognition. (Regulation 18901, copy enclosed.) Regulation 18901(c) (2) (ii) sets out the general rule that a mass mailing sent out by an elected officer may not include the elected officer's name, office, photograph or other reference to the elected officer. Regulation 18901(f) lists exceptions to the general rule. specifically, Regulation 18901(f) (8) provides an exception for: (8) Mailings sent to the elected officer's constituents which directly relate to that elected
